stories.title: New PR review experience with parallel execution
stories.summary: Cursor 0.43 (marketed as 'Cursor 3') ships two distinct changes: a redesigned PR review UI and parallel plan execution via async subagents. The PR review overhaul adds inline comment threads, a commit history timeline, and a file-tree navigator — addressing usability gaps that made large PRs (50+ files) painful to navigate inside the editor. The parallel execution engine identifies independent tasks within a plan and dispatches them concurrently as async subagents rather than running sequentially, with a 'Build in Parallel' toggle and the option to split work across multiple PRs. The net result: multi-component refactors or feature builds that previously blocked on sequential agent steps can now fan out, meaning a task that spawned 4 sequential subagent calls could complete in roughly 1/4 the wall-clock time assuming no inter-task dependencies.
stories.considerations: The concrete win is in multi-service refactors: if you're updating an API contract that touches a backend handler, frontend client, tests, and docs independently, parallel subagents can work all four simultaneously instead of waiting for each to finish — what might have been a 4-minute sequential agent run could drop to ~1 minute. The non-obvious risk is subagent write conflicts: if the dependency graph is misidentified and two agents touch the same file or shared type, you get a merge conflict or silent overwrite inside the agent's working context, which is harder to debug than a sequential failure. This lands at a moment when the main user complaint about AI coding agents is latency — sequential execution makes even simple multi-file tasks feel slow, and parallelism directly attacks that bottleneck. The honest gotcha: 'Build in Parallel' likely consumes significantly more tokens and fast-premium requests simultaneously, so teams on metered plans should expect to burn through request quotas faster, and the 'split into multiple PRs' flow adds review overhead that may negate time savings for smaller teams.

stories.title: Devin Review and Quick Review in Windsurf
stories.summary: Windsurf has added two code review modes — Devin Review and Quick Review — directly inside the editor. Devin Review appears to invoke a more thorough, agent-driven analysis (likely multi-pass, similar to Devin's autonomous verification loops), while Quick Review targets fast inline feedback for smaller changesets. Both run inside the Windsurf workspace, eliminating the context switch to a separate review tool or browser-based PR interface. Developers can now trigger code verification against their working tree without leaving the editor, meaning review feedback is available before a commit or PR is opened.
stories.considerations: The most concrete win here is for solo developers or small teams doing self-review: instead of pushing a draft PR to GitHub and waiting for CI plus a manual pass, you can get structured feedback on a 50-line function change in-editor before it ever leaves your machine. The non-obvious implication is that embedding review at write-time rather than PR-time shifts the feedback loop earlier in the SDLC — if Devin Review flags architectural issues, you catch them before branch divergence makes them expensive to fix. This lands at a moment when AI-assisted coding is generating code faster than human review cycles can absorb it, so in-editor verification is a direct answer to the 'AI writes it faster than we can review it' bottleneck teams are hitting right now. The honest gotcha: the excerpt gives zero detail on what Devin Review actually checks — static analysis, semantic correctness, security patterns, style — so it's unclear whether this is substantive agent-driven verification or a rebranded linter; until that's documented, treat the 'Devin' branding skeptically and test it against a real bug class you care about.

stories.title: gpt-realtime-2 model release
stories.summary: OpenAI released gpt-realtime-2, a new model available across three endpoints: v1/realtime (bidirectional audio), v1/realtime/translations (live audio translation), and v1/realtime/transcription_se (dedicated transcription, likely Speech Engine variant). The model upgrade applies to WebSocket-based streaming sessions where audio is processed server-side in real time rather than batched. The dedicated transcription and translation endpoints are new splits from the original monolithic realtime endpoint, suggesting specialized model routing per task type. Developers can now build translation pipelines and transcription services on the realtime infrastructure without routing through the general-purpose audio session endpoint.
stories.considerations: A concrete scenario: if you're building a multilingual support bot that previously had to chain Whisper transcription → GPT-4o text → TTS synthesis, v1/realtime/translations collapses that into a single WebSocket session, cutting at least two round-trip latencies and eliminating the intermediate text representation entirely. The non-obvious implication is that splitting transcription and translation into dedicated endpoints hints at separate fine-tuned model weights per task — meaning prompt strategies or system instructions that worked on the original realtime endpoint may not transfer cleanly and will need re-validation. This matters now because real-time voice AI is hitting a wall where latency from chained REST calls (typically 800ms–2s per hop) makes conversational UX feel broken, and a unified streaming endpoint is the architectural fix the ecosystem has been waiting for. The honest gotcha: 'gpt-realtime-2' version pinning is good for stability, but the changelog gives zero detail on context window size, audio token pricing, or whether existing gpt-4o-realtime-preview sessions auto-migrate — budget and capacity planning is currently a black box until OpenAI publishes the model card.

stories.title: Filter session history by workspace directory
stories.summary: Continue.dev added workspace-directory filtering to session history, letting users scope the chat history panel to sessions from a specific project folder rather than viewing all sessions globally. The filter works by associating each session with the workspace root active at session creation time, then surfacing only matching sessions when a filter is applied. No new API or config changes are required — it's a UI-level filter on existing session metadata. Developers juggling multiple repos can now pull up only the sessions relevant to the project they're currently in, without manually scanning a flat list of mixed-context conversations.
stories.considerations: The most direct win is for developers who context-switch frequently — say, between a monorepo and a separate infrastructure repo — and previously had to scroll past dozens of irrelevant sessions to find a conversation about a specific service's auth logic. The non-obvious implication: session metadata is now implicitly tied to workspace root paths, which means renaming or moving a project directory will likely orphan those sessions from the filter, making older history invisible unless you know to clear the filter. This addresses a real friction point as Continue usage scales — once you accumulate hundreds of sessions across projects, a flat history becomes nearly useless for retrieval. Worth noting that this is purely a client-side filter on existing data, so there's no migration needed, but also no retroactive re-association if your workspace paths have changed since those sessions were created.

stories.title: Claude Code Hooks: Synchronous Middleware for Every Tool Call
stories.summary: Claude Code now exposes a formal hooks system that intercepts every tool call synchronously inside the agentic loop — no monkey-patching, no forking internals. Hooks are shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts registered at 20+ named lifecycle points (SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, and others), receiving full JSON context via stdin or POST body and returning structured decisions Claude treats as authoritative. The critical mechanism: exit code 2 is a first-class signal that injects a blocking message directly into Claude's context window, allowing a hook to say 'that path is protected, try another' and trigger replanning — not just halt execution. Developers can now enforce org-wide guardrails (protected directories, secret scanning, schema validation) that Claude actively reasons around rather than blindly crashing into.
stories.considerations: In a 300-file monorepo where Claude is doing autonomous refactoring, a PreToolUse hook on Write/Edit tools can check every target path against a protected-files manifest and return exit code 2 with a specific redirect message — Claude will replan the write to a staging path rather than failing the whole session, which previously required either manual supervision or accepting silent failures. The second-order effect worth thinking through: exit code 2 hooks make Claude's behavior contingent on your hook's latency, so a slow HTTP endpoint or a chained LLM-prompt hook sitting in the critical path of every tool call can meaningfully degrade session throughput — this isn't async middleware, it's synchronous blocking. This lands at exactly the right moment because agentic Claude Code sessions are getting longer and touching more sensitive infrastructure, and the previous answer to 'how do I enforce invariants' was 'write it in the system prompt and hope' — hooks make enforcement structural rather than advisory. The gotcha: hooks are configured per-user in ~/.claude/settings.json or per-project, which means enterprise teams need a separate distribution and enforcement story to ensure hooks are actually present on every developer's machine — there's no server-side hook registration, so a developer who clones a repo without the hooks config gets no protection.
